agent1_snippets.title: McCune-Albright Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
agent1_snippets.content: McCune-Albright syndrome is a rare genetic disordered originally recognized by the triad of polyostotic fibrous dysplasia, precocious puberty, and cafe-au-late spots. A variety of endocrine disorders, including hyperthyroidism, acromegaly, phosphate wasting, and Cushing syndrome are now considered as part of the endocrinopathies seen in this disorder. The variable constellation of symptoms arises from a somatic activating mutation of the GNAS gene, which is present in many tissue types. This activity reviews the etiology, presentation, evaluation, and management of McCune-Albright syndrome and reviews the role of the interprofessional team in evaluating, diagnosing, and managing the condition.

agent1_snippets.title: Gigantism and Acromegaly -- Continuing Education Activity
agent1_snippets.content: Acromegaly and gigantism are disorders of growth hormone hypersecretion. The most common cause is a growth hormone (GH) secreting adenoma in the pituitary gland. Gigantism occurs when growth hormone hypersecretion occurs before the fusion of the long bone epiphysis and is characterized by tall stature. Acromegaly occurs when GH hypersecretion occurs after the fusion of the epiphysis leading to large extremities and characteristic facies. An elevated insulin-like growth factor-1 (IGF-1) level establishes the diagnosis. The first line of treatment is surgical excision of the tumor; however, this rarely results in a cure, and further medical treatment with somatostatin analogs or radiation is usually necessary. This activity explains the evaluation and management of gigantism and acromegaly and emphasizes the role of the interprofessional team in improving the care for patients with this condition.

agent1_snippets.title: McCune-Albright Syndrome -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: McCune-Albright syndrome is a rare genetic disordered originally recognized by the triad of polyostotic fibrous dysplasia, precocious puberty, and café-au-lait spots. A variety of endocrine disorders, including hyperthyroidism, acromegaly, phosphate wasting, and Cushing syndrome are now considered as part of the endocrinopathies seen in this disorder. The variable constellation of symptoms arises from a somatic activating mutation of the GNAS gene, which is present in many tissue types. [1]

agent1_snippets.title: McCune-Albright Syndrome -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: Treatment of hyperthyroidism is initially managed medically with antithyroid medications and radioablation.  Growth hormone excess treatment is by somatostatin or direct GH receptor antagonists. Hyperprolactinemia management is with bromocriptine.  In patients with demonstrated pituitary adenomas, surgical resection of the adenoma is an option.  Treatment of hypophosphatemia with oral supplementation is under debate, but supplementation should be used in the setting of rickets. [7] [8]

agent1_snippets.title: Acromegaly -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
agent1_snippets.content: Acromegaly is not a common disorder, but it is associated with very high morbidity and mortality rates when it presents. Because the presentation of acromegaly is systemic, an interprofessional team approach is necessary.

agent1_snippets.title: Peripheral Precocious Puberty -- Etiology -- McCune-Albright syndrome
agent1_snippets.content: McCune Albright Syndrome is a rare condition that can present with irregular café au lait spots, polyostotic fibrous dysplasia, and peripheral precocious puberty in girls. Estrogen overproduction in this condition leads to early breast development, vaginal bleeding, recurrent follicular cysts, and accelerated bone growth. At times, vaginal bleeding may precede the onset of breast development. McCune-Albright syndrome can also present with endocrinopathies such as thyrotoxicosis, Cushing syndrome, gigantism, or acromegaly. [8]

agent1_snippets.title: McCune-Albright Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
agent1_snippets.content: An increase in intracellular cAMP signaling is responsible for the clinical manifestations of McCune-Albright syndrome. The phenotype is dependent on which tissue types the mutation occurs. [2] In bone, increased cAMP causes osteoblasts to differentiate into stromal cells while inhibiting further differentiation, resulting in fibrous dysplasia. These fibrous dysplastic lesions then exhibit increased secretion of phosphaturic hormone fibroblastic growth factor-23 (FGF23), causing renal phosphate wasting. [3] Increased cAMP signaling in the skin results in stimulation of melanin production via alpha-MSH, resulting in café-au-lait macules. Increased cAMP in endocrine tissue results in increased production and secretion of a specific hormone product.  Affected endocrine tissues can include the gonads, thyroid, pituitary, parathyroid, and adrenal glands. [1]

agent1_snippets.title: Acromegaly -- Etiology
agent1_snippets.content: Other causes of primary GH excess include pituitary adenomas that secrete multiple hormones and GH-cell carcinomas. Important familial syndromes associated with acromegaly include Multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1, familial acromegaly, McCune-Albright syndrome, and Carney complex. GH excess can also be ectopic and produced by other tumors such as lymphoma and pancreatic-islet cell tumors. GH excess can also be iatrogenic, resulting from excessive GH administration.

agent1_snippets.title: McCune-Albright Syndrome -- Prognosis
agent1_snippets.content: Prognosis may be challenging to ascertain due to the varying severity of the disease. Younger patients with renal phosphate wasting and polyostotic lesions have a higher risk of clinically relevant bone pain and fracture. [12] The primary treatment goal of precocious puberty is the preservation of potential adult height. Without intervention, growth plates close prematurely resulting in short stature. [9] While rare, there is potential for malignant transformation of fibrous dysplastic lesions. [3] There are also reports of increased breast cancer risk. [1]
agent1_snippets.query_used:

agent1_snippets.title: McCune-Albright Syndrome -- Evaluation
agent1_snippets.content: Evaluation for growth hormone excess includes oral glucose tolerance test and serum growth hormone and prolactin measurements.  Evaluation of hyperthyroidism includes measurement of thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH), free and bound thyroxine, and T3. Periodic ultrasound is recommended with an appropriate follow-up of any abnormal findings. Monitoring levels of serum phosphate and renal reabsorption of phosphate is also a recommendation. [7] [8]

agent1_snippets.title: Gigantism and Acromegaly -- History and Physical
agent1_snippets.content: Acromegaly is a very insidious disease where changes occur over many years. Consequently, studies have reported that the diagnosis is often delayed for about nine years on average. Patients may seek care for dental problems and cardiac or rheumatological issues before being referred to endocrinology.

agent1_snippets.title: Fibrous Dysplasia -- History and Physical
agent1_snippets.content: Patients with monostotic fibrous dysplasia are often asymptomatic.  Occasionally patients may present with bone pain. Pathologic fracture with minor inciting trauma can also be a first presentation. [10] Pregnancy can increase disease activity and therefore increase the risk of pain and pathologic fracture. [11] History and physical examination may contribute little to disease evaluation in cases of asymptomatic osseous involvement. History should address the characteristics of bone tenderness, bony protuberance, osseous asymmetry, endocrine disturbance, and dermatologic complaints. A family history of bone lesions may be present in hereditary forms of fibrous dysplasia such as cherubism. Although malignant transformation is rare, it does occur in less than 1% of cases. [12] In a patient with known fibrous dysplasia, periodic evaluation for aggressive lesion growth should be performed. [13] McCune-Albright syndrome should be considered in the setting of hyperfunctioning endocrinopathy.

agent1_snippets.content: Three primary modalities are available for treating acromegaly, each with its advantages and disadvantages: surgery, medical therapy, and radiation. The decision to use these modalities is made on a case-by-case basis.

agent1_snippets.title: Fibrous Dysplasia -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
agent1_snippets.content: Managing fibrous dysplasia involves an interprofessional team approach, primarily with pediatrics/primary care, orthopedics, and radiology.  Genetics and endocrinology should be added for cases of McCune-Albright syndrome. Craniofacial involvement should prompt neurology, ophthalmology, audiology, and possible neurosurgery consultation. [3] [36] Additional support from physical therapy and psychiatry in cases of disability or deformity may be warranted. [3]

agent1_snippets.title: Fibrous Dysplasia -- Continuing Education Activity
agent1_snippets.content: Fibrous dysplasia is a typically benign bone lesion characterized by intramedullary fibro-osseous proliferation secondary to altered osteogenesis. The polyostotic form often presents in childhood. Malignant transformation is rare, and remote radiation therapy has been reported as a risk factor. McCune-Albright syndrome is a relatively rare condition featuring polyostotic fibrous dysplasia (often unilateral) with skin pigmentation lesions and endocrine dysfunction (often female precocious puberty). This activity examines how to properly evaluate fibrous dysplasia and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in caring for patients with this condition.

agent1_snippets.title: Gigantism and Acromegaly -- Pearls and Other Issues
agent1_snippets.content: Acromegaly and gigantism are due to the oversecretion of growth hormone. The most common cause is a GH-secreting pituitary adenoma. Rarely, ectopic GH secretion or excess secretion of GHRH may be the cause.

agent1_snippets.title: Gigantism and Acromegaly -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
agent1_snippets.content: Untreated acromegaly has increased mortality compared to the general population. The major contributors to mortality are cardiovascular disease (60%), respiratory disease (25%), and malignancies (15%). Mortality rates are similar to the general population in patients with controlled GH secretion. Besides the level of GH, the presence of cardiac disease is an important prognostic indicator. The presence of cardiac disease at diagnosis has a 100% mortality rate at 15 years. The mortality rate of patients with acromegaly and diabetes at 20 years is 80%. [40] [41]

agent1_snippets.title: Acromegaly -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: Acromegaly is a rare disorder caused by excessive growth hormone production (GH), most commonly from an adenoma of the anterior pituitary gland. The resulting production of insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF-1) causes the characteristic overgrowth of certain tissues resulting in coarsening of facial features, enlarging hands and feet, as well as effects on multiple systems throughout the body, including cardiovascular, rheumatologic, neurologic, pulmonary, neoplastic, and metabolic (see Image. Acromegaly, gigantism). Appropriate screening and early diagnosis are important to hinder associated morbidity and mortality related to the disease by treating early and the involvement of a multidisciplinary team. [1] [2] [3] [4]

agent1_snippets.title: Gigantism and Acromegaly -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: Acromegaly and gigantism are disorders of growth hormone hypersecretion (see Image. Acromegaly, Gigantism). The most common cause is a growth hormone (GH) secreting adenoma in the pituitary gland. Gigantism occurs when growth hormone hypersecretion occurs before the fusion of the long bone epiphysis and is characterized by tall stature. Acromegaly occurs when GH hypersecretion occurs after the fusion of the epiphysis leading to large extremities and characteristic facies. An elevated insulin-like growth factor-1 (IGF-1) level establishes the diagnosis. The first line of treatment is surgical excision of the tumor; however, this rarely results in a cure, and further medical treatment with somatostatin analogs or radiation is usually necessary. [1] [2]

agent1_snippets.content: Besides control of GH, the management of acromegaly involves the treatment of acromegalic complications, which include hypertension, cardiomyopathy, OSA, colonic polyps, hypopituitarism, and arthropathy.

agent1_snippets.title: Acromegaly -- Continuing Education Activity
agent1_snippets.content: Acromegaly is a disorder caused by excessive growth hormone production from the anterior pituitary gland, resulting in excessive growth of body tissues and other metabolic dysfunctions. The causes of acromegaly can be divided into primary GH excess, ectopic or iatrogenic GH excess, and excess growth hormone-releasing hormone (GHRH). Adult patients with acromegaly have the characteristic facial features of a large lower jaw, a prominent forehead, and large hands and feet. This occurs after the growth plates are fused, distinguishing acromegaly from gigantism, which occurs before the fusion of growth plates. This activity reviews the evaluation and management of acromegaly and highlights the interprofessional team's role in providing care to patients affected by this condition.

This classification guides diagnostic evaluation, treatment planning, and prognostic assessment within the context of endocrine pathology.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1267
agent2_snippets.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a broad category that includes several specific pancreatic endocrine disorders. Among its children are ارتفاع السكر في الدم, which describes the elevated blood‑glucose levels that characterize hyperglycemia; مرض السكري دون مضاعفات, which refers to diabetes that has not yet produced secondary complications; فرط أنسولين الدم, indicating an excess of circulating insulin often seen in early insulin‑resistance states or insulin‑producing tumors; and ورم غلوكاغوني, a rare glucagon‑secreting tumor (glucagonoma) that can also cause hyperglycemia. These conditions are interrelated through their impact on pancreatic hormone production and regulation of blood glucose, highlighting the importance of comprehensive assessment and management of pancreatic endocrine function in patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1930
agent2_snippets.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a broad classification that encompasses several distinct conditions. Within this category, مرض السكري دون مضاعفات refers to diabetes without complications, representing a form of the disease where hyperglycemia is present but secondary complications have not yet developed. فرط أنسولين الدم denotes hyperinsulinemia, a state of elevated insulin levels in the blood that can arise from pancreatic beta‑cell overactivity or insulin‑secreting tumors and may lead to hypoglycemia. ورم غلوكاغوني describes glucagonoma, a rare pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor that secretes excess glucagon, causing characteristic metabolic disturbances such as glucose intolerance, weight loss, and a distinctive skin rash. Together, these three entities illustrate the spectrum of disorders that fall under DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES, highlighting the varied clinical presentations and management considerations associated with pancreatic endocrine pathology.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1875
agent2_snippets.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a broad category that includes several related disorders: ارتفاع السكر في الدم (elevated blood glucose), مرض السكري دون مضاعفات (diabetes without complications), اعتلال عصبي سكري (diabetic neuropathy), فرط أنسولين الدم (hyperinsulinemia), and ورم غلوكاغوني (glucagonoma). These entities represent a spectrum of pancreatic endocrine dysfunction, ranging from simple hyperglycemia to specific complications such as neuropathy, as well as hormonal excess states like hyperinsulinemia and glucagon‑secreting tumors. Understanding that all of these conditions are subtypes of diseases of the endocrine pancreas, including diabetes, helps clinicians recognize the varied presentations, anticipate potential complications, and tailor comprehensive patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2841
agent2_snippets.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a classification that encompasses several distinct disorders of pancreatic endocrine function. It includes مرض السكري دون مضاعفات, which denotes diabetes mellitus without any associated complications and represents the basic metabolic disturbance of chronic hyperglycemia. It also comprises اعتلال عصبي سكري, a chronic diabetic neuropathy in which prolonged hyperglycemia damages peripheral nerves, leading to sensory loss, pain, and autonomic dysfunction. The category further lists ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي, a progressive atrophy of one side of the facial tissues that can be observed in some patients with endocrine pancreatic disease. فرط أنسولين الدم is another child condition, describing an excess of circulating insulin that may arise from insulin‑producing tumors (insulinoma) or other dysregulation of pancreatic β‑cell activity. Finally, ورم غلوكاغوني refers to a glucagon‑secreting pancreatic tumor (glucagonoma) that causes hyperglycemia and a characteristic clinical syndrome. Together, these entities illustrate the full spectrum of pancreatic endocrine disorders—from uncomplicated diabetes to its neuropathic complications, hormonal excess states, and neoplastic growths—underscoring the need for comprehensive evaluation and targeted management in patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1136
agent2_snippets.content: Arthropathy associated with an endocrine AND/OR metabolic disorder is a joint‑related disease that is identified in the klouby (joints). Surgical procedures described as incision and excision of joint structures also pertain to the klouby, indicating that both the pathological manifestation of endocrine‑ or metabolic‑related arthropathy and its operative management are centered on joint anatomy. This underscores the clinical relevance of recognizing endocrine or metabolic contributions to joint disease and the importance of joint‑focused surgical interventions in patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2175
agent2_snippets.content: Triiodothyronine (trijodthyronin) is a thyroid hormone that is synthesized and secreted by the thyroid gland (štítná žláza). The štítná žláza is also the primary anatomical site where thyroid insufficiency (قصور الدَّرَقِيَّة), a condition characterized by reduced production of thyroid hormones, is identified. Recognizing the relationship between trijodthyronin and the štítná žláza, as well as the gland’s role in قصور الدَّرَقِيَّة, is essential for accurate diagnosis and effective management of disorders affecting thyroid hormone metabolism.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2046
agent2_snippets.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES encompasses a spectrum of related conditions. It includes ارتفاع السكر في الدم, which denotes elevated blood glucose levels, and مرض السكري دون مضاعفات, representing diabetes without complications. Neurological involvement is reflected by اعتلال عصبي سكري, a diabetic neuropathy. Facial manifestations may appear as ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي, a progressive hemi‑facial atrophy that can be associated with pancreatic endocrine disorders. Metabolic dysregulation is further illustrated by فرط أنسولين الدم, an excess of circulating insulin, and by ورم غلوكاغوني, a glucagon‑producing tumor of the pancreas. Together these entities illustrate the diverse clinical presentations and complications that arise from diseases of the endocrine pancreas, highlighting the importance of comprehensive assessment and management in affected patients.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2721
agent2_snippets.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a broad category that includes ارتفاع السكر في الدم, اعتلال عصبي سكري, فرط أنسولين الدم, and ورم غلوكاغوني. ارتفاع السكر في الدم (high blood sugar) is a primary manifestation of diabetes, reflecting impaired glucose regulation by the endocrine pancreas. اعتلال عصبي سكري (diabetic neuropathy) is a common chronic complication of prolonged hyperglycemia, affecting peripheral nerves and leading to sensory and motor deficits. فرط أنسولين الدم (hyperinsulinemia) denotes excessive insulin secretion, which can occur in early insulin resistance or insulin‑producing tumors and may precede or accompany diabetes. ورم غلوكاغوني (glucagonoma) is a rare pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor that secretes excess glucagon, often resulting in persistent hyperglycemia and weight loss. Recognizing how these conditions are interrelated within endocrine pancreatic disease supports comprehensive diagnosis, monitoring, and therapeutic strategies for affected patients.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1970
agent2_snippets.content: The category “DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES” is positioned within the broader group of “other endocrine disorders” and encompasses a range of specific pancreatic and metabolic conditions. Among its sub‑entities are “مرض السكري دون مضاعفات” (diabetes without complications), “نقص تركيز السكر” (hypoglycemia), “اعتلال عصبي سكري” (diabetic neuropathy), “ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي” (progressive hemifacial atrophy), “فرط أنسولين الدم” (hyperinsulinemia), and “ورم غلوكاغوني” (glucagonoma). Hypoglycemia is therefore recognized both as a distinct manifestation under the diabetes‑related pancreatic disease spectrum and as a condition that belongs to the larger classification of other endocrine disorders. Collectively, these entities illustrate the diverse clinical presentations that can arise from endocrine dysfunction of the pancreas, highlighting the importance of recognizing both isolated metabolic disturbances and their broader endocrine context for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient management.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 194
agent2_snippets.content: The classification “DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES” is situated within the broader category of “other endocrine disorders.” Within this group, several specific conditions are identified as subtypes or manifestations: “ارتفاع السكر في الدم” (hyperglycemia) represents elevated blood glucose levels; “مرض السكري دون مضاعفات” denotes diabetes mellitus without complications; “نقص تركيز السكر” (hypoglycemia) reflects abnormally low blood glucose and is also recognized as a component of the wider “other endocrine disorders” category; “فرط أنسولين الدم” (hyperinsulinemia) describes excessive circulating insulin; “ورم غلوكاغوني” (glucagonoma) is a pancreatic tumor that secretes glucagon; and “اضطراب إفراز الجسترين” (gastrin secretion disorder) indicates abnormal gastrin release. The gastrin secretion disorder further leads to “الحثل وإضطرابات الإفراز في الغشاء المخاطي المَعِدِيّ المِعَوِيّ” (gastric mucosal atrophy and associated secretion abnormalities), illustrating a downstream effect on the gastric mucosa. Together, these relationships delineate how diabetes‑related endocrine pancreatic diseases encompass a spectrum of hyper‑ and hypoglycemic states, hormone‑excess conditions, neoplastic processes, and secondary gastrointestinal disturbances, all nested within the larger framework of endocrine disorders.
